prompt:
Single-frame image in the visual tradition of 19th-century entomological naturalist plates (Victorian scientific illustration, c. 1840-1890), rendered as a hand-colored lithographic study sheet. Depict gradient descent through an invented moth species, Noctua optimensis, shown in dorsal and ventral views with precise wing venation. The wing patterns encode contour lines of a convex loss landscape, with concentric rings narrowing toward the thorax as the global minimum. Around the main specimen, include 8-10 small developmental studies arranged as field notes: larva positions x0 to x8 moving downhill along curved terrain cross-sections, each with tiny serif labels and reduced loss values. Add one lower-corner analytic inset titled Iteratio Descensus showing two paths: stable convergence and oscillatory overshoot, both mapped as natural-history measurement sketches rather than modern infographics. Composition: sparse cream paper, plate number, thin ruled margins, copperplate-like linework, stipple shading, restrained hand tinting (olive, burnt sienna, faded ultramarine, smoke gray), authentic museum-catalog tone, no anachronistic UI elements or modern typography.

Negative prompt: photorealism, glossy digital rendering, sans-serif fonts, neon colors, modern UI, 3D gradients, contemporary infographic layout, anime, low detail, blurry, watermark, signature, frame split, multi-panel
Parameters: width=1280, height=1600, num_inference_steps=8
tradition_approach: Used dorsal/ventral specimen studies, copperplate-like line and stipple, restrained hand-colored lithographic tinting, ruled plate margins, and museum-catalog annotation style to encode iterative descent and convergence as period scientific observation.
